citation.authors: Jia, L., Alva, K. S., Li, H., Hager, W., Romero, L., Butler, T. A., & Lewis, M.
citation.title: Understanding Situation Awareness in Multi-UAV Supervision: Effects of Environmental Complexity, Interface Design, and Human–AI Differences

extraction_metadata.notes: This is the exact Jia et al. (2026) article the Study-Type codebook's own v0.2 Change Log cites as the motivating test case for the Setting sub-attribute's Simulated/Remote default rule (a 393-participant online/browser-based study with no VR headset) — that rule is applied directly here. Re-coded 2026-07-10 against Task-Environment v0.8: the search/comprehension relation now maps onto the new Search Task (4.2) code, replacing the original Unclear / Candidate New Code flag.

study_type.justification: A fully-powered, formal 4x2 mixed-design experiment (393 of 400 recruited participants, pre-registered power analysis, linear mixed-effects models) evaluating object-specific and global situation awareness is the article's sole and central contribution; the exploratory GPT-4o comparison is conducted within the same experimental report rather than as a separate system contribution, so no Secondary code is needed.

summary.task: Participants supervised six simulated UAVs conducting a 10-minute wildfire-reconnaissance mission over a simulated forested region (modeled on California's Cleveland National Forest), each UAV scripted to fly a fixed left-to-right path while autonomously flagging salient targets (hikers, vehicles, wildfires, and deliberately inserted false alarms). Participants clicked each flagged target to review zoomed footage and annotate its identity/status (e.g., movement direction, rescue need), and periodically answered paused, memory-based 'global' questions summarizing everything observed across all UAVs in a region.
summary.control: Participants never flew or repositioned the UAVs — vehicles autonomously followed pre-scripted trajectories and autonomously flagged salient objects — so the only operator action was informational/interpretive: clicking a flagged target or map icon to open zoomed footage and complete an annotation. Four between-subjects interface variants manipulated this access: a Synchronous interface (zoomed footage viewable only while the target remained in a live UAV's current field of view), an ASynchronous interface (a persistent map icon let users revisit a target's footage at any time), and Ablated versions of each that removed the continuous live-video panel entirely, leaving only on-demand map-marker access.
summary.participants: 400 US-based adults recruited via the CloudResearch platform (100 per interface condition); 393 completed the study and provided usable data (mean age 36.43, SD 10.85; 200 male, 200 female). A pre-registered power analysis (G*Power, f = 0.15, alpha = .05, power = .95) indicated a minimum of 272 participants were needed. Each participant completed one ~30-minute session for $5 compensation. A separate exploratory arm evaluated OpenAI's GPT-4o on the same object-specific and global SA questions under matched conditions, without inferential statistics given the model's single deterministic response per prompt.
summary.results: Across all interface types, participants were significantly more accurate on immediate, object-specific (SPAM-based) queries than on memory-based global (SAGAT-based) queries (p < .001), and accuracy declined significantly as SA level increased from perception to comprehension to projection, especially under high target density. The Synchronous interface produced significantly lower object-specific SA accuracy and significantly more missed-target annotations than all three other interfaces; interfaces supporting retrospective access (ASynchronous) or reduced visual clutter (Ablated variants) helped maintain SA, though global SA accuracy was far less affected by interface type. Whether a wildfire appeared before or after related targets significantly shaped global SA accuracy across SA levels. GPT-4o matched humans on object-specific Level-1 (perception) but underperformed on Levels 2-3 (comprehension/projection), while it outperformed humans across all three levels on memory-intensive global queries, indicating complementary rather than uniformly superior/inferior AI performance.
summary.issues: Users' most common self-reported challenges (open-ended feedback, inter-rater kappa = .91) were: memory/recall burden (no way to revisit the map or past footage), fast task pace/time pressure (new targets appearing before an annotation could be finished), cognitive overload from simultaneous multitasking (monitoring, identifying, judging risk, and annotating at once), and difficulty making directional/dynamic judgments (e.g., whether a hiker was moving toward a moving fire) with no clear directional cues. Memory-load and time-pressure complaints concentrated in the Synchronous and Synchronous-Ablated (non-persistent-access) conditions, while multitasking-overload and directional-judgment complaints were more common in the Asynchronous conditions. The human-LLM comparison is explicitly exploratory (a single, general-purpose, non-fine-tuned LLM in deterministic mode, no inferential statistics), and participants were drawn from the general public rather than professional UAV/wildfire operators.
summary.recommendations: Reduce memory demands and support continuous comprehension via persistent-access structural elements (snapshot panels, collapsible history timelines, dynamic filters) plus simple annotation/progress-tracking tools. Design SA support to adapt dynamically to evolving task complexity (target density, event precedence, target type), e.g., using behavioral/physiological signals (eye movements, fixation duration) to predict SA state and proactively highlight uninspected areas. Pursue hybrid human-AI supervisory division of labor that assigns real-time perceptual interpretation and high-stakes decisions to humans while offloading memory-intensive tracking, multi-target summarization, and pattern-based anomaly detection to AI/LLM agents. Validate findings with expert/professional UAV operators and more ecologically realistic data, and examine fine-tuned LLMs and deployment factors like latency and trust calibration.
summary.autonomous_capabilities: Each of the six UAVs autonomously flew a fixed, pre-scripted flight path across the forested area and autonomously detected/flagged salient objects of interest (a red bounding box overlay plus a corresponding map marker), without any operator control over flight path, obstacle avoidance, or target detection itself. All higher-order reasoning (object identity, risk assessment, projected behavior) was deliberately delegated entirely to the human (or, in the exploratory arm, to GPT-4o); the system provided zoomed video and spatial/temporal metadata but performed no interpretation of its own.

task_object_spatiotemporal_dynamics.justification: 45 targets (4 wildfires, 13 vehicles, 23 hikers, 5 false alarms) are pre-placed at scattered fixed locations across nine columns of the forested area, each a separate, self-contained instance encountered as the UAVs sweep across.

Hikers and vehicles are given 'context-appropriate animations to simulate plausible motion and behavior,' and SA questions explicitly ask about a target's movement direction and whether it is approaching the wildfire, requiring the operator to track ongoing relocation rather than a one-off static assessment.

behavioral_relation_to_task_object.justification: The operator's task is to detect, identify, and assess the risk/status of targets — a search-and-comprehension relation, not avoidance — matching Section 4.2 (Search Task), added in v0.8.

team_size.justification: Each participant individually supervises the same six-UAV fleet.

team_composition.justification: All six UAVs are identical in capability and behavior, differing only in assigned flight lane.

interaction_model.justification: A single operator simultaneously monitors and interacts with video feeds and flagged targets from all six UAVs.

interaction_directness.justification: Operators click explicitly on system-flagged targets or map markers to open footage and submit annotations.

communication_proximity.justification: The entire study is browser-based and online (WebGL build deployed via Netlify, recruited through CloudResearch), with no physical UAVs or co-location.

coordination_architecture.justification: Each of the six UAVs is explicitly described as independently following its own fixed, pre-scripted flight path side by side, with no negotiation, leader, or shared decision-making described among them — coordination is absent by design, not merely undescribed.

computational_characteristics.justification: Both UAV flight ('scripted to follow a fixed flight path') and target flagging are pre-programmed/scripted simulation behaviors rather than a computed optimization, search, or learned model — matching fixed, predetermined action sequences.

human_involvement_level.justification: UAV flight and target-flagging execute autonomously and relay their output to the operator, who monitors but whose input is not required for these functions to operate.

All higher-order reasoning — identity, risk, and projection judgments — is explicitly and entirely delegated to the human (or the LLM); the system performs no interpretation of its own, so this function cannot complete without that input.

level_of_human_control_abstraction.justification: Selecting a flagged target and submitting an annotation are discrete, one-off inputs; the system has already done all the work of detecting and presenting the target.

The study logs participants' continuous camera movements while reviewing footage, matching this codebook's established precedent (Scope note, v0.7) that continuous view/payload manipulation is coded Direct/Augmented and dominant under Rule 2 even while an autonomous function runs in the background.

operational_domain.justification: All six vehicles are UAVs operating in the air domain.

flags.note: Surfaced per the shared application guide even though the determination is clean: the six UAVs are explicitly independent and non-coordinating by design.
